Name: infotheoreticrateoptimization
Title: Optimizing Model Compression through Information-Theoretic Rate-Distortion Analysis
Short Hypothesis: By framing model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive optimal bit allocations for different layers of a neural network that minimize the distortion while preserving task-relevant information. This approach will provide a principled way to achieve high compression ratios without significant loss in model performance.
Related Work: Recent works such as 'Probabilistic Automated Model Compression via Representation Mutual Information Optimization' and 'SNIP: An Adaptive Mixed Precision Framework for Subbyte Large Language Model Training' have explored the use of mutual information and adaptive precision for model compression. However, these methods often lack a clear theoretical foundation for the rate-distortion trade-off and do not provide a systematic way to derive optimal bit allocations. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by providing a rigorous information-theoretic framework for model compression.
Abstract: We propose a novel information-theoretic approach to model compression that optimizes the rate-distortion trade-off for neural networks. By framing compression as a lossy source coding problem, we derive optimal bit allocations for different layers of the network that minimize the distortion while preserving task-relevant information. Our method uses mutual information and entropy estimators to quantify the information content of each layer and allocates bits accordingly. We validate our approach on several benchmark datasets and models, demonstrating significant improvements in compression efficiency without a significant loss in performance. This work provides a principled framework for model compression and offers insights into the fundamental limits of compressibility.

Experiments.Name: Bit Allocation Optimization
Experiments.Description: We will derive the optimal bit allocation for each layer of a neural network using a rate-distortion optimization framework. This involves estimating the mutual information and entropy of each layer's activations and gradients, and then using these estimates to allocate bits in a way that minimizes the overall distortion.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: We will evaluate the performance of the compressed model using accuracy on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) and the compression ratio (bits per weight). We will also compare the rate-distortion trade-off curves against state-of-the-art compression methods.

Experiments.Name: Sensitivity Analysis
Experiments.Description: We will perform a sensitivity analysis to understand how the optimal bit allocation changes with different hyperparameters and model architectures. This will help us identify the most critical layers for compression and the robustness of our method to variations in the model.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: We will measure the change in accuracy and compression ratio when varying the hyperparameters and model architectures. We will also analyze the impact of different distortion measures on the final compression performance.

Experiments.Name: Generalization to Different Tasks
Experiments.Description: We will test the generalizability of our method to different tasks, including image classification, object detection, and natural language processing. This will help us understand the applicability of our approach across a range of model types and datasets.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: We will evaluate the performance of the compressed models on various tasks using task-specific metrics (e.g., F1 score for object detection, perplexity for language models) and compare the results to the uncompressed models.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Accurate estimation of mutual information and entropy can be challenging, especially for high-dimensional data. We will use state-of-the-art estimators and validate their performance on synthetic datasets.
The optimal bit allocation may vary across different tasks and datasets, requiring careful tuning and validation. We will conduct extensive experiments to ensure the robustness of our method.
The computational cost of deriving the optimal bit allocation can be high, especially for large models. We will explore efficient algorithms and approximations to reduce the computational burden.
